entries.title: Portsmouth Housing Authority (PHA) — Overview
entries.body:
**Founded:** 1953. HUD-recognized high-performing agency.

**Scale:**
- 650+ apartments across 12 properties
- 1,700+ residents housed
- 460+ Housing Choice Vouchers (Section 8)

**Structure:** Two entities:
1. Portsmouth Housing Authority (PHA) — owns/operates
2. PHA Housing Development Ltd. — affiliated nonprofit, develops using LIHTC + CDFA credits

**Key contact:** Craig Welch, Executive Director — craigwelch@nh-pha.com / 603-436-4310

**Mission:** Quality, affordable housing for low- and moderate-income residents through fiscally responsible, creative organization.

entries.title: Key Projects
entries.body:
**Ruth Griffin Place**
- 82 units, ~$1,000/month rents
- Built on city-owned land with private capital + federal tax credits
- Tabor calls it 'the template for success' — proof that below-market housing can be built well in Portsmouth's core

**Sherburne School**
- 127 affordable units
- Former city-owned elementary school
- Originally proposed at 80-160 units, cut to 80-100 after Pannaway neighborhood pushback, then scrapped entirely before being revived
- Finally crossed finish line — Tabor's signature win

**Lafayette Road / Christ Church**
- 44 workforce housing units
- Also includes HAVEN domestic violence HQ + Little Blessings Childcare
- Funded partly by CDFA tax credits ($193k FY2026)
- Model for church/nonprofit surplus land partnerships

entries.title: Gateway Neighborhood Overlay District (GNOD)
entries.body:
**What it is:** Overlay district passed 2024 covering Commerce Way and Portsmouth Boulevard — formerly office-research zoned land.

**What it allows:**
- Higher density housing and workforce housing
- Up to 6 stories
- Up to 120 dwelling units per building
- Bonus incentives for affordability

**Scale:** ~23 acres, walkable to downtown and grocery stores

**Land transfer option:** Could create 80+ units for PHA or similar nonprofit developers

**Quote from Tabor:** 'Regardless of how it got here, the discussions led to a lot of innovative ideas and innovative zoning that's going to allow us to boost the city housing stock in a location that's not going to have a lot of neighborhood impact.'

**Significance:** Proof of concept for the overlay model — Tabor wants to replicate this approach for other underutilized zones.

entries.title: Housing Action Plan — Four Pillars
entries.body:
Framework Tabor described — council tasking committee to turn advocate recommendations into executable plan.

**1. Remove Zoning Barriers**
- Audit ordinance for parking minimums, setbacks, use restrictions, density caps
- Identify quick wins
- Feed into 2027 zoning rewrite
- Study Nashua and other NH cities on enabling legislation
- Build 'barriers register' — living doc tracking obstacles + status

**2. Put More Land Into Use**
- Inventory city-owned parcels (Sherburne = proof of concept)
- Identify surplus land from nonprofits/churches (Lafayette Rd = model)
- Map underutilized commercial/office zones (GNOD = template)
- Prioritize walkable sites

**3. Increase Density Strategically**
- Differentiate by neighborhood — higher near downtown/commercial, gentler (ADUs, duplexes) in residential like Pannaway
- ADU streamlining
- 50/50 market rate / affordable model (Montgomery County MD template)
- GNOD as replicable overlay tool

**4. Deploy Financial Tools**
- Tax abatements (sunset when affordability covenants expire)
- Tax deferrals for low-income homeowners
- LIHTC / CDFA credits via PHA Housing Development Ltd.
- Housing Trust Fund — ensure active deployment, push for dedicated staff
- HUD/Section 8 optimization — voucher utilization + landlord participation
- Benchmark against comparable Seacoast/NH markets

**Cross-cutting: Data & Accountability**
- Baseline metrics: units/year, waitlist trend, voucher utilization, cost/unit
- Demand signals: emergency assistance requests rose 28 → 81 between July–Sept 2024
- Regular public reporting

entries.title: Council Strategic Goals (Housing)
entries.body:
**Goal summary:** Increase supply, decrease cost, simplify process, expand below-market options.

**Strategic objectives:**
- Create a Homeowners Bill of Rights (by end of 2027)
- Rewrite the zoning ordinance (by end of 2027)
- Create a Housing Action Plan including review of former Housing Navigator plans (by July 2026)
- Implement the Housing Action Plan (by end of 2027)

**Target:** Tabor has stated Portsmouth needs ~1,500 units in 2 years to balance the market.
